prerequisites: An Amazon Redshift cluster (or Serverless workgroup) that is reachable from the connector, with its endpoint hostname known for the host field.
The target database name on the cluster (database field) and a schema in that database to write into (schema field).
The cluster port that the database listens on (port field). Amazon Redshift uses 5439 by default per the example JDBC URL jdbc:redshift://{endpoint}:{port}/{database}.

A Redshift database user (username) and its password (password) with permission to write to the target schema. Per the GRANT reference, this means USAGE and CREATE on the schema and INSERT on the destination tables (INSERT also covers loading via the COPY statement).

If staging through Amazon S3 (uploading_method with access keys), an AWS access key id and secret access key whose IAM identity can read/write the staging S3 bucket so Redshift COPY can load the data.
If the cluster is only reachable through a bastion host, SSH tunnel details for tunnel_method (the SSH key or tunnel user password).

authMethods.name: Redshift database username and password
authMethods.summary: Authenticate to the cluster with a Redshift database user (username config field) and its password (password secret field). These map to the JDBC UID and PWD properties; when the server does not use SSL, the username and password alone authenticate the connection.

authMethods.name: AWS access keys for S3 staging (uploading_method)
authMethods.summary: When loading via an S3 staging bucket, provide uploading_method.access_key_id and uploading_method.secret_access_key. These map to the COPY command's ACCESS_KEY_ID and SECRET_ACCESS_KEY authorization parameters used to load data from S3 into Redshift.

authMethods.name: SSH tunnel credentials (tunnel_method)
authMethods.summary: Optional. When the cluster is reached through an SSH bastion, supply the tunnel connection via tunnel_method, using either an SSH private key (tunnel_method.ssh_key) or a tunnel user password (tunnel_method.tunnel_user_password). This secures the network path and is independent of the Redshift database login.

setupSteps.title: Collect the cluster connection details
setupSteps.body: From the Redshift cluster (or Serverless workgroup), get its endpoint and use it for host, the listening port for port (5439 by default), the database name for database, and the target schema for schema. The JDBC URL form jdbc:redshift://{endpoint}:{port}/{database} shows how these combine.

setupSteps.title: Create or choose a database user and grant write permissions
setupSteps.body: Provide username and password for a Redshift user. Using GRANT, give that user USAGE and CREATE on the target schema and INSERT on the destination tables so it can create tables and load rows (INSERT covers loading via the COPY statement). Use REVOKE to scope down access as needed.

setupSteps.title: Configure the staging upload method (optional)
setupSteps.body: If using S3 staging, set uploading_method and supply uploading_method.access_key_id and uploading_method.secret_access_key. Redshift uses these as the COPY command ACCESS_KEY_ID and SECRET_ACCESS_KEY parameters to read staged files from S3. AWS notes this key-based method is less recommended than IAM roles, so use credentials whose IAM identity has only the required S3 access.

setupSteps.title: Configure an SSH tunnel if required (optional)
setupSteps.body: If the cluster is not directly reachable, set tunnel_method to route through a bastion host and provide either tunnel_method.ssh_key (SSH private key) or tunnel_method.tunnel_user_password.

setupSteps.title: Set optional connection tuning fields
setupSteps.body: Optionally set jdbc_url_params to append extra JDBC connection parameters, and drop_cascade to control cascading drops during sync operations. Note that any URL values containing reserved characters such as ; + { } [ ] & = ? or spaces must be URL-encoded.

sources.title: Getting the JDBC URL - Amazon Redshift
